questions.q: What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding?
options: Overloading occurs in the same class with different parameters; overriding occurs in derived classes with same signature
Overloading and overriding are the same concept with different names
Overriding can only happen with private methods
Overloading requires inheritance while overriding does not
questions.explanation: Overloading creates multiple methods with the same name but different parameters in a single class (compile-time polymorphism), while overriding replaces a base class method in a derived class (runtime polymorphism).

questions.q: What is dynamic dispatch in object-oriented programming?
options: The compiler determining which method to call at compile time
The runtime selecting the appropriate method based on the actual object type
A technique for speeding up method calls
A requirement that all classes must inherit from a base class
questions.explanation: Dynamic dispatch is when the runtime determines which implementation of a method to call based on the actual type of the object, not its declared type. This enables polymorphic behavior.

questions.q: In C++ or Java, what does the 'virtual' (or overrideable) keyword enable?
options: Faster execution of methods
Dynamic dispatch so derived classes can override base class methods
Automatic memory management
Compilation of the code without warnings
questions.explanation: Virtual methods enable dynamic dispatch, allowing derived classes to override methods and the runtime to call the correct implementation based on the actual object type rather than the reference type.

questions.q: How does an interface support polymorphism?
options: Interfaces prevent polymorphism by forcing strict typing
Multiple classes can implement the same interface, allowing them to be used interchangeably through the interface reference
Interfaces only work with primitive types
Interfaces automatically call methods without user intervention
questions.explanation: Interfaces define a contract that multiple classes can implement, enabling polymorphic behavior where different implementations can be used through a common interface reference.

questions.q: Given a Shape interface with a draw() method, and Circle, Square, and Triangle classes implementing it, what is the benefit of polymorphism?
options: All shapes must be drawn at the same speed
You can call draw() on a Shape reference and the appropriate implementation executes regardless of the actual type
The interface automatically creates shapes for you
Polymorphism only works if all shapes have the same number of sides
questions.explanation: Polymorphism allows you to work with Shape references and call draw() on them, and the correct implementation for Circle, Square, or Triangle executes at runtime without needing type checks.

questions.q: What is a potential problem with improper method overloading?
options: It always improves code clarity and readability
Overloading with similar parameter types can create confusion and unexpected behavior
Overloading prevents inheritance from working correctly
Overloaded methods always have better performance
questions.explanation: Overloading with subtle parameter type differences (e.g., int vs Integer, or int vs long) can lead to unexpected method selection and confusing behavior, especially with implicit type conversions.

questions.q: When overriding a method in a derived class, what must remain consistent with the base class method?
options: The method must have a different name to distinguish it
The method signature (name, parameters) must match, and the return type should be compatible (covariant)
The method must be slower to execute than the base version
The method must always perform identical behavior
questions.explanation: Override methods must have the same signature to properly override the base class method. Return types can be covariant (a subtype of the original), but the method name and parameters must match.

questions.q: Consider a Payment system with a process() method in CreditCard, PayPal, and Bitcoin classes. All are used through a Payment interface. If one implementation throws an exception unexpectedly, what polymorphic principle might be violated?
options: The Liskov Substitution Principle, since callers expect any Payment to behave consistently
Overloading rules, which prevent exception handling
Virtual method requirements, which forbid polymorphic behavior
Interface implementation rules that require identical exception signatures
questions.explanation: If a Payment implementation behaves differently than others (e.g., throwing unexpected exceptions), it violates LSP because code using the Payment interface assumes consistent behavior.

questions.q: What is the relationship between polymorphism and type checking at runtime?
options: Type checking prevents polymorphism from functioning
Polymorphism eliminates the need for any type checking
Runtime type checking enables the dispatch mechanism to call the correct method
Type checking only works at compile time, never at runtime
questions.explanation: At runtime, the type checking system (through virtual method tables or similar mechanisms) determines the actual type of an object and dispatches to the correct method implementation.

questions.q: Why might using 'instanceof' checks extensively to determine which subclass you're working with indicate a design flaw?
options: instanceof is always faster than polymorphism
It defeats the purpose of polymorphism by relying on compile-time knowledge of types instead of runtime dispatch
instanceof checks require classes to be in the same file
Polymorphism and instanceof serve identical purposes
questions.explanation: Excessive instanceof checks indicate you're treating polymorphic objects as concrete types, defeating polymorphism. Proper design should use method overriding and dynamic dispatch instead.
